[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Surat_Thani_town_hall.jpg/250px-Surat_Thani_town_hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surat_Thani_town_hall.jpg)

**Thesaban** ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เทศบาล, [RTGS](/wiki/Royal_Thai_General_System_of_Transcription "Royal Thai General System of Transcription"): *thetsaban*, pronounced [[tʰêːt.sā.bāːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai"), [Pali](/wiki/Pali_language "Pali language"): *desapāla*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'protector of region') are the municipalities of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). There are three levels of municipalities: city, town, and sub-district. Bangkok and Pattaya are special municipal entities not included in the *thesaban* system.

The municipalities assume some of the responsibilities which are assigned to the districts (*[amphoe](/wiki/Amphoe "Amphoe")*) or subdistricts (*[tambon](/wiki/Tambon "Tambon")*) for non-municipal (rural) areas. Historically, this [devolution](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") of central government powers grew out of the [Sukhaphiban](/wiki/Sukhaphiban "Sukhaphiban") (สุขาภิบาล) [sanitary districts](/wiki/Sanitary_district "Sanitary district") first created in Bangkok by a royal decree of King [Chulalongkorn](/wiki/Chulalongkorn "Chulalongkorn") in 1897.

The *thesaban* system was established in the Thesaban Organization Act of 1934 ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): พระราชบัญญัติจัดระเบียบเทศบาล พุทธศักราช ๒๔๗๖),[[1]](#cite_note-1934_act-1) and has been updated several times since, starting with the Thesaban Act of 1939 ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): พระราชบัญญัติเทศบาล พุทธศักราช ๒๔๘๑),[[2]](#cite_note-1939_act-2) which was replaced by the Thesaban Act of 1953.[[3]](#cite_note-1953_act-3) The 1953 act was most recently amended by the Thesaban Act (No. 12) of 2003.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## City municipality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thesaban&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: City municipality")]

*Thesaban nakhon* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เทศบาลนคร, [RTGS](/wiki/Royal_Thai_General_System_of_Transcription "Royal Thai General System of Transcription"): **thetsaban nakhon**, pronounced [[tʰêːt.sā.bāːn ná(ʔ).kʰɔ̄ːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")) is translated as "city municipality". To qualify for city status a municipality needs to have a population of at least 50,000 and sufficient income to carry out the tasks of a city.

When first organized in 1934, the minimum qualifications for city status were a population of 30,000 with a density of 1,000 per km2.[[1]](#cite_note-1934_act-1) In 1939 the required population density was increased to 2,000 per km2, along with the addition of a financial requirement.[[2]](#cite_note-1939_act-2) In 1953 the population density requirement was again raised, to 3,000 per km2,[[3]](#cite_note-1953_act-3) before being removed entirely in 2000.[[5]](#cite_note-2000_act-5)

For 22 years, from 1972 to 1994, as well as between March and November 1936, there was only one city municipality in Thailand, which was [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai"), as in 1972 [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") had been changed from city municipality, to special governed district. Until 1972 there were three city municipalities: [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai"), [Phra Nakhon](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") (now Bangkok), and [Thonburi](/wiki/Thonburi "Thonburi") (now part of Bangkok). In 1994, [Nakhon Si Thammarat](/wiki/Nakhon_Si_Thammarat "Nakhon Si Thammarat") town municipality became the second city municipality of Thailand, and the first in the south.

## Town municipality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thesaban&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Town municipality")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/67/Banpong_Municipality_Seal.png)](/wiki/File:Banpong_Municipality_Seal.png)

Banpong Municipality Logo

*Thesaban mueang* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เทศบาลเมือง, [RTGS](/wiki/Royal_Thai_General_System_of_Transcription "Royal Thai General System of Transcription"): **thetsaban mueang**, pronounced [[tʰêːt.sā.bāːn mɯ̄a̯ŋ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")) is translated as "town municipality". For a municipality to qualify as a town, it either needs to be a provincial capital, or have a population of at least 10,000 and sufficient income to cover the tasks of a town.

When first organized in 1934, the minimum qualification for town status was a population of 3,000 with a density of 1,000 per km2.[[1]](#cite_note-1934_act-1) In 1939 requirements were increased to a population of 5,000 with a density of 2,000 per km2, plus a financial criterion.[[2]](#cite_note-1939_act-2) In 1953, the minimum population requirements was raised to the present value; the population density was also raised, to 3,000 per km2,[[3]](#cite_note-1953_act-3) before being removed entirely in 2000.[[5]](#cite_note-2000_act-5)

## Subdistrict municipality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thesaban&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Subdistrict municipality")]

*Thesaban tambon* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เทศบาลตำบล, [RTGS](/wiki/Royal_Thai_General_System_of_Transcription "Royal Thai General System of Transcription"): **thetsaban tambon**, pronounced [[tʰêːt.sā.bāːn tām.bōn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")), the lowest level municipal unit, is usually translated as "sub-district municipality". Despite the name, it may not necessarily cover the same area as a sub-district (*[tambon](/wiki/Tambon "Tambon")*); i.e., it may not cover a *tambon* completely, or conversely, it may extend over parts of more than one *tambon*.

For an area to qualify as a *thesaban tambon*, it must have a gross income of at least 5 million [baht](/wiki/Baht "Baht") and a population of at least 5,000 with a minimum density of 1,500 per km2, plus the approval of the population within that area.[[6]](#cite_note-Definition-6)

Many of today's *thesaban* municipalities were originally *[sukhaphiban](/wiki/Sukhaphiban "Sukhaphiban") tambon*, sanitation districts, the number of which had grown to 35 in 1935, when these were converted into municipalities. New sanitary districts were again established starting in 1952. With the *Act to Upgrade Sanitary Districts to Thesaban* of May 1999 all were converted in May 1999,[[7]](#cite_note-7) though many of them did not actually meet the criteria above.
